It is not for us to object against the people's smarting for the sin of their king; perhaps they helped him. Nor against this generation suffering for the sin of the last. God often visits the sins of the fathers upon the children, and he gives no account of any matters. Time does not wear out the guilt of sin; nor can we build hopes of escape upon the delay of judgments. If we cannot understand all the reasons of Providence in this matter, still we have no right to demand that God should acquaint us with those reasons. It must be right, because it is the will of God, and in the end it will be proved to be so. Money is no satisfaction for blood. It should seem, Saul's posterity trod in his steps, for it is called a bloody house. It was the spirit of the family, therefore they are justly reckoned with for his sin, as well as for their own. The Gibeonites did not require this out of malice against Saul or his family. It was not to gratify any revenge, but for the public good. They were put to death at the beginning of harvest; they were thus sacrificed to turn away the wrath of Almighty God, who had withheld the harvest-mercies for some years past, and to obtain his favour in the present harvest. In vain do we expect mercy from God, unless we do justice upon our sins. Executions must not be thought cruel, which are for the public welfare.
21.m:
Every problem comes from sin and should make us feel sorry and humble ourselves before God. But sometimes trouble shows that it’s meant to remind us of our sins. 

God’s punishments often look far back in time, which means we need to think about our past mistakes too. We shouldn’t complain if people suffer because of what their leaders did wrong. Maybe the people supported the leader’s bad actions. Or if one group suffers because of what another group did wrong before them, that’s fair too. 

God often punishes children for their parents’ sins, and He doesn’t always explain His reasons. Time doesn’t erase guilt. And waiting long for punishment doesn’t mean you’ll get off free. Even if we don’t fully understand God’s ways, we shouldn’t ask Him to explain everything. What He decides is right, even if we don’t see why.

Money can’t fix the harm done by killing someone. Saul’s family continued his bad habits, making them guilty too. So they’re punished for both their own sins and Saul’s. 

The Gibeonites didn’t want revenge on Saul or his family. Their request was for the good of everyone. They killed these men at the start of harvest season. This sacrifice turned away God’s anger, which had caused poor harvests for several years. Without doing justice on our sins, we can’t hope for God’s mercy. 

Executions that help the community aren’t cruel, even if they seem harsh.

21.o: That a guilty land should enjoy many years of plenty, calls for gratitude; and we need not wonder misused abundance should be punished with scarcity; yet how few are disposed to ask of the Lord concerning the sinful cause, while numbers search for the second causes by which he is pleased to work! But the Lord will plead the cause of those who cannot or will not avenge themselves; and the prayers of the poor are of great power. When God sent rain to water the earth, these bodies were buried, for then it appeared that God was entreated for the land. When justice is done on earth, vengeance from heaven ceases. God is pacified, and is entreated for us through Christ, who was hanged on a tree, and so made a curse for us, to do away our guilt, though he was himself guiltless.
21.m:
A guilty land should have plenty of good times to feel thankful. It’s not surprising that if people misuse their blessings, they might face shortages later. Yet, how rare it is for someone to ask God about the real reason behind these changes, instead of just looking at the natural reasons He uses.

But God takes up the case for those who can’t or won’t fight back. The prayers of the needy are very powerful. 

When God sends rain to help the crops grow, the dead bodies get covered over. This shows that God has been asked to help the land. 

Once justice happens here on Earth, God stops sending punishment from above. God becomes peaceful again and listens to us because of Christ. Jesus died on a cross to take away our sins, even though He didn’t deserve it.

21.o: These events seem to have taken place towards the end of David's reign. David fainted, but he did not flee, and God sent help in the time of need. In spiritual conflicts, even strong saints sometimes wax faint; then Satan attacks them furiously; but those who stand their ground and resist him, shall be relieved and made more than conquerors. Death is a Christian's last enemy, and a son of Anak; but through Him that triumphed for us, believers shall be more than conquerors at last, even over that enemy.
21.m:
These events likely happened near the end of David’s reign. David felt weak, but he didn’t run away. Instead, God sent help when David needed it most. 

In times of spiritual struggle, even strong believers can feel weak. Then Satan tries to attack them fiercely. But if they stay firm and fight back, God will come to their aid and help them win.

Death is a Christian’s final enemy, much like Goliath was for David. Yet, because Jesus won the battle for us, believers will ultimately overcome death too.

23.o: These words of David are very worthy of regard. Let those who have had long experience of God's goodness, and the pleasantness of heavenly wisdom, when they come to finish their course, bear their testimony to the truth of the promise. David avows his Divine inspiration, that the Spirit of God spake by him. He, and other holy men, spake and wrote as they were moved by the Holy Ghost. In many things he had his own neglect and wrong conduct to blame. But David comforted himself that the Lord had made with him an everlasting covenant. By this he principally intended the covenant of mercy and peace, which the Lord made with him as a sinner, who believed in the promised Saviour, who embraced the promised blessing, who yielded up himself to the Lord, to be his redeemed servant. Believers shall for ever enjoy covenant blessings; and God the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost, shall be for ever glorified in their salvation. Thus pardon, righteousness, grace, and eternal life, are secured as the gift of God through Jesus Christ. There is an infinite fulness of grace and all blessings treasured up in Christ, for those who seek his salvation. This covenant was all David's salvation, he so well knew the holy law of God and the extent of his own sinfulness, that he perceived what was needful for his own case in this salvation. It was therefore all his desire. In comparison, all earthly objects lost their attractions; he was willing to give them up, or to die and leave them, that he might enjoy full happiness, Ps 73:24–28. Still the power of evil, and the weakness of his faith, hope, and love, were his grief and burden. Doubtless he would have allowed that his own slackness and want of care were the cause; but the hope that he should soon be made perfect in glory, encouraged him in his dying moments.
23.m:
David’s words are worth listening to. People who have known God’s kindness for a long time should speak about its truth when they near the end of their lives. 

David said that God spoke through him. The Holy Spirit guided him and other prophets to write and speak God’s message. 

David admitted that he sometimes did wrong things because of his own mistakes. But he found comfort knowing that God made an everlasting covenant with him. 

This covenant meant that God forgave David’s sins and gave him peace. As someone who trusted in the coming Savior, David saw this as a special promise. 

Believers will always receive these promises from God. And God the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit will be honored forever through our salvation. 

Through Jesus Christ, we get forgiveness, righteousness, grace, and eternal life. All the good gifts of God are stored up in Christ for those who seek His salvation. 

For David, this covenant was everything he needed. Knowing God’s laws and recognizing his own sins helped him see how much he needed this salvation. So, having this salvation became his greatest desire. 

Compared to worldly things, nothing else mattered anymore. He was ready to let go of everything if it meant enjoying true happiness, as seen in Psalm 73:24–28. 

Even though David struggled with temptation and doubted at times, he looked forward to being perfected in heaven. This hope kept him strong until the end.

23.o: David once earnestly longed for the water at the well of Bethlehem. It seems to be an instance of weakness. He was thirsty; with the water of that well he had often refreshed himself when a youth, and it was without due thought that he desired it. Were his valiant men so forward to expose themselves, upon the least hint of their prince's mind, and so eager to please him, and shall not we long to approve ourselves to our Lord Jesus, by ready compliance with his will, as shown us by his word, Spirit, and providence? But David poured out the water as a drink-offering to the Lord. Thus he would cross his own foolish fancy, and punish himself for indulging it, and show that he had sober thoughts to correct his rash ones, and knew how to deny himself. Did David look upon that water as very precious which was got at the hazard of these men's blood, and shall not we much more value those benefits for purchasing which our blessed Saviour shed his blood? Let all beware of neglecting so great salvation.
23.m:
David once really wanted some water from the well in Bethlehem. This seemed like a moment of weakness. He was thirsty and remembered drinking from that well when he was young. Without thinking carefully, he wished for the water.

His brave soldiers were always willing to do what he asked, even if it meant danger. They tried hard to please him. Shouldn’t we want to please Jesus just as eagerly by following his wishes, as shown through his Word, Spirit, and actions?

But David poured the water out as an offering to God. By doing this, he showed he understood his desire wasn’t wise. He corrected his mistake and learned to control himself.

David saw the water as very special because his men risked their lives to get it. We should see Jesus’ sacrifice as even more valuable since he gave his life to buy our salvation.

Everyone needs to be careful not to miss such a wonderful chance to find true salvation.

24.o: For the people's sin David was left to act wrong, and in his chastisement they received punishment. This example throws light upon God's government of the world, and furnishes a useful lesson. The pride of David's heart, was his sin in numbering of the people. He thought thereby to appear the more formidable, trusting in an arm of flesh more than he should have done, and though he had written so much of trusting in God only. God judges not of sin as we do. What appears to us harmless, or, at least, but a small offence, may be a great sin in the eye of God, who discerns the thoughts and intents of the heart. Even ungodly men can discern evil tempers and wrong conduct in believers, of which they themselves often remain unconscious. But God seldom allows those whom he loves the pleasures they sinfully covet.
24.m:
For the people's sins, David did wrong things, and because of his punishment, they were punished too. This shows how God rules the world and gives us a helpful lesson. 

David’s pride led him to count the people, thinking it would show how powerful he was. He trusted in human strength instead of God, even though he wrote about trusting only in God. 

God sees sin differently from us. Something that seems small to us might be a big sin to God, especially if it comes from bad intentions. Ungodly people can see bad attitudes and behavior in believers that those believers don’t notice. 

But God doesn't usually let the ones he loves enjoy the sinful things they desire.

24.o: It is well, when a man has sinned, if he has a heart within to smite him for it. If we confess our sins, we may pray in faith that God would forgive them, and take away, by pardoning mercy, that sin which we cast away by sincere repentance. What we make the matter of our pride, it is just in God to take from us, or make bitter to us, and make it our punishment. This must be such a punishment as the people have a large share in, for though it was David's sin that opened the sluice, the sins of the people all contributed to the flood. In this difficulty, David chose a judgment which came immediately from God, whose mercies he knew to be very great, rather than from men, who would have triumphed in the miseries of Israel, and have been thereby hardened in their idolatry. He chose the pestilence; he and his family would be as much exposed to it as the poorest Israelite; and he would continue for a shorter time under the Divine rebuke, however severe it was. The rapid destruction by the pestilence shows how easily God can bring down the proudest sinners, and how much we owe daily to the Divine patience.
24.m:
When someone has done wrong, it’s good if they feel sorry about it inside. We can ask God to forgive us if we admit our mistakes. God will forgive us through His kindness if we truly regret what we did.

Whatever makes us proud, God can take away or turn against us as punishment. Even though King David’s sin started things, everyone else’s sins helped cause the problem too.

In his trouble, David picked a punishment directly from God because he knew God’s mercy was great. He didn’t want human judges who might celebrate Israel’s suffering and become even more stubborn in their worship of idols.

David chose the plague. It would affect him and his family just as much as anyone else. And he would suffer God’s displeasure for a shorter time, no matter how harshly.

The quick spread of the plague shows how easily God can humble the most arrogant sinners. We should thank God each day for being patient with us.

24.o: Perhaps there was more wickedness, especially more pride, and that was the sin now chastised, in Jerusalem than elsewhere, therefore the hand of the destroyer is stretched out upon that city; but the Lord repented him of the evil, changed not his mind, but his way. In the very place where Abraham was stayed from slaying his son, this angel, by a like countermand, was stayed from destroying Jerusalem. It is for the sake of the great Sacrifice, that our forfeited lives are preserved from the destroying angel. And in David is the spirit of a true shepherd of the people, offering himself as a sacrifice to God, for the salvation of his subjects.
24.m:
Maybe there was more wickedness, especially more pride, which was the sin being punished at that time in Jerusalem compared to other places. So, the Lord sent trouble to that city. But then he decided not to punish them anymore. 

Just like when an angel stopped Abraham from sacrificing his son, here another angel was told to stop destroying Jerusalem. Our lives are saved from the angel who brings death because of the great sacrifice Jesus made. 

David showed the heart of a good leader when he offered to take the punishment himself to save his people.

24.o: God's encouraging us to offer to him spiritual sacrifices, is an evidence of his reconciling us to himself. David purchased the ground to build the altar. God hates robbery for burnt-offering. Those know not what religion is, who chiefly care to make it cheap and easy to themselves, and who are best pleased with that which costs them least pains or money. For what have we our substance, but to honour God with it; and how can it be better bestowed? See the building of the altar, and the offering proper sacrifices upon it. Burnt-offerings to the glory of God's justice; peace-offerings to the glory of his mercy. Christ is our Altar, our Sacrifice; in him alone we may expect to escape his wrath, and to find favour with God. Death is destroying all around, in so many forms, and so suddenly, that it is madness not to expect and prepare for the close of life.
24.m:
God encourages us to give him spiritual offerings, showing he wants to reconcile with us. David bought the land to build the altar. God doesn’t want stolen things as burnt offerings. People don't truly understand religion if they only choose the easiest and cheapest ways. We should use our resources to honor God. How else could we spend them better?

The altar was built, and proper sacrifices were offered on it. Burnt offerings show God’s justice. Peace offerings show his mercy. Jesus is our altar and sacrifice. Only through him can we avoid God’s anger and receive his favor.

Death comes in many sudden ways, so it makes sense to get ready for the end of life.
